status: Content authored but not deployed in MVP-1 baseline. Available as opt-in alternative once engineering can swap the framing layer per user preference. Documented as a remaining gap in concept.md Draft 0.2; this file closes the gap at the content layer.
rationale: The default inventory framing ('I am a person who...') is structurally individualist: it presupposes that moral life is constituted by personal traits to be developed. MacIntyre's After Virtue argues this is incoherent absent tradition-constituted communities, and Confucian role-ethics (Ames 2011, Rosemont & Ames 2009) holds that moral life is constituted by relational roles rather than personal qualities. For users from those traditions — and arguably for most users some of the time — the 'as a [role], I am someone who...' framing is closer to how moral identity actually operates. This variant offers that framing as an opt-in alternative without forcing it on users for whom the default works.

philosophical_anchor.primary: Ames, R. T. (2011). Confucian Role Ethics: A Vocabulary.
supporting: Rosemont, H. & Ames, R. (2009). The Chinese Classic of Family Reverence.
Hursthouse, R. (1999). On Virtue Ethics. (Aristotelian middle position.)

role_taxonomy.selection_protocol: User picks 1–3 most-salient roles at onboarding. Each selected role runs through the inventory protocols (card-sort, pairwise, story prompts) with the role-specific framing wrappers. The default trait-framing is also available — these are complementary, not exclusive.

roles.label: As a direct report / team member
As a citizen of my country
As a member of my tradition / faith / culture

role_taxonomy.ux_notes: Tradition / faith / culture is a single combined option to respect users whose self-identification is non-religious cultural. The role list is presented as a card-pick where the user selects 1–3; ordering is randomized except for the family category which leads (most users have at least one family role and the lead-position reduces selection fatigue).

current_self.relational_anchor_phrase: As a {role}, I am someone who...
current_self.card_sort_prompt: Pick the five values that most describe how you actually act in your role as a {role}. Not how you want to act — how you actually do.
current_self.pairwise_prompt: As a {role}, when you can't fully have both — which do you actually lean toward?

aspirational_self.default_anchor_phrase: I want to be a person who...
aspirational_self.relational_anchor_phrase: As a {role}, I want to become someone who...
aspirational_self.card_sort_prompt: Pick the five values that describe who you want to become in your role as a {role}.
aspirational_self.pairwise_prompt: As a {role}, when you can't fully have both — which would the version of yourself you'd want to become lean toward?

admired_other.relational_anchor_phrase: Someone I deeply respect, in their role as a {role}, is someone who...
admired_other.card_sort_prompt: Bring to mind someone you deeply respect who serves well in the {role} role. Pick the five values that describe how they actually act in that role.
admired_other.pairwise_prompt: Thinking of the person you brought to mind — as a {role}, when they can't fully have both, which do they actually lean toward?

story-pride.default: Tell me about a time, recently, when you were quietly proud of how you handled something.
story-pride.relational: Tell me about a time, recently, when you were quietly proud of how you handled something *in your role as a {role}*. Two or three sentences is plenty.

story-regret.default: Tell me about a time, recently, when you let yourself down.
story-regret.relational: Tell me about a time, recently, when you didn't live up to what you owe *as a {role}*. Not catastrophically — a small moment when you knew you fell short of the role.

story-anticipated-test.default: Is there a situation coming up that you think will test you in some way?
story-anticipated-test.relational: Is there a situation coming up that will test you specifically *in your role as a {role}*? Just describe it briefly.

story-admired-model.default: Tell me one specific thing the person you deeply respect does that earns that respect.
story-admired-model.relational: Tell me one specific thing the person you respect *does in their role as a {role}* that earns that respect.

story-aspirational-target.default: Imagine the version of yourself you'd most like to be in five years.
story-aspirational-target.relational: Imagine the version of yourself you'd most like to be in five years *in your role as a {role}*. Name one specific way they'd act that you don't, yet.

scoring_implications.summary: Relational scores are computed per role × per layer × per domain, in parallel to the default (role-free) scoring. The analyzer treats role-tagged data as a richer view of the same construct, not a competing one.
scoring_implications.primary_revealed_score: Unchanged. Revealed scores come from scenario behavior, which is not role-conditioned in MVP-1 (scenarios are written without role-anchoring; the user is making the choice as themselves).
scoring_implications.stated_scores: Each role generates its own stated-values vector. The user's *overall* stated score is the mean across their selected roles (mean-rather-than-max is the conservative choice; if a user picks fewer roles, the overall reflects fewer averaging steps but the analytic pipeline doesn't down-weight them).
scoring_implications.gap_calculation: Gap can be computed per role for users who selected the relational variant — this is genuinely additional information, since a user might have a small gap as 'colleague' and a large one as 'partner', and the practice would be most useful focused on the role with the larger gap. MVP-1 reports only the role-averaged gap to avoid surfacing detail that hasn't been validated for stability yet.
scoring_implications.validation_caveat: The relational variant is *not* in the MVP-1 pre-registration. It ships as data-collection-enabled only if engineering enables the framing layer; analyses of role-conditioned data are reported only as exploratory. Pre-registration for the role-conditioned hypotheses is a Phase-2 study.

implementation_notes.mvp_1: Author the content (this file). Do not enable the role-selection UI by default. Engineering may stub the role-picker behind a feature flag for internal testing.
implementation_notes.mvp_2: Role-picker available as opt-in setting; user can switch between trait-default and relational-variant framings at any time. Both protocols coexist; user-conditional surfacing.
implementation_notes.future_localization: Some translations (especially East Asian languages and traditions) may default to the relational variant rather than treating it as opt-in. Translation work should make this localization decision explicitly per locale.
implementation_notes.engineering_dependencies: Requires a template-string substitution layer that the rest of the inventory engine doesn't currently need. Adding it for this feature is justified; reusing it for other content (e.g., counterparty templating in scenarios) is a natural extension.

open_questions: Whether the default 'I am a person who...' framing should itself be reframable as a 'free' role ('as a person living in the world'). Doing so would unify the abstractions but might confuse users who are happy with the trait framing.
Whether to surface role-specific feedback in the profile reveal during MVP-2. Per-role gap is informative but might be overwhelming; alternative is averaged with optional drill-in.

Whether the role taxonomy should be culturally localized. Roles like 'as a citizen of my country' don't translate cleanly across all cultures; 'as a member of my caste' or 'as the eldest child' may be needed for some.
Whether to allow free-text custom roles. Probably yes long-term, with LLM-coding to the canonical taxonomy for analysis; risk of fragmentation in the validation cohort suggests no for Phase-1.
